legend(x="topleft",legend=c(expression(F[w] == bar(F[-w])%*% abs(~chi(0.75+I%*%2%*%pi%*%exp(2%*%w)) )),""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.6)
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e4,1e4),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of spliced filtered F(w)_EP1 + \n scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP1)",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.5,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e4,1e4))
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transform of the first Riemann-Siegel Euler Product component and its scaled mirror conjugate.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.62)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e4,1e4),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of filtered F(w)_EP1",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend="NEW splicing method",bg="white",xjust=0)


```

\clearpage


```{r Zfnzetafunctionoveraninterval_anditsfouriertransform_26140_pm819_resp, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="A comparison of the \\textbf{real part} of the fourier transforms of the Riemann-Siegel Z functions of the Riemann Zeta function and partial Euler Product (using truncation at $N_1 = \\sqrt{\\frac{t}{2\\pi}}$) in the complex plane at $\\sigma=0.25$ for the interval t=26140+(-819.1,819.2). The first row shows an existing method to filter and splice together the \\textbf{real parts} of the two fourier transform components of the partial Euler Product approximation of the Riemann-Siegel Z function. This old method has reasonable accuracy for $\\sigma=0.75$ as can be seen with little visual difference in the middle column result compared to the true fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function shown in the middle row. The bottom row uses the zeroth order functional relationship between the fourier sidebands of the Riemann-Siegel Z function to provide a robust partial Euler Product approximation whereby (since $\\sigma=0.25 < 0.5$) only the \\textbf{real part} of the filtered second fourier transform component is spliced together with the \\textbf{real part} of a scaled mirror conjugate of itself.", out.width = c("99%"), fig.show = "hold",  fig.height=7.5, warning=FALSE}

df=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p25_26140_Z.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
df$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",df$V1));
df$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",df$V2));

dg=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p25_26140_zeta.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dg$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dg$V1));
dg$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dg$V2));

dh=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p25_26140_EP12b.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dh$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dh$V1));
dh$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dh$V2));

di=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p25_26140_fadjneg.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
di$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",di$V1));
di$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",di$V2));

dj=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p25_26140_fhEP12b.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dj$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dj$V1));
dj$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dj$V2));

dk=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p25_26140_EP12a.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dk$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dk$V1));
dk$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dk$V2));

par(mar = c(1.75, 2.05, 1.5, 1.5))
# plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1)),c(as.numeric(df$V1)[16385:32768],as.numeric(df$V1)[1:16384]),col="gray",typ="l",    xlim=c(-2500,2500),ylim=c(-3e5,3e5));grid(col="gray2")

layout(mat = matrix(c(1,2,3,
                      4,5,6,
                      7,8,9),
                    nrow = 3,
                    byrow = TRUE))

plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[16385:32768],(dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend="old splicing method",bg="white",xjust=0)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c(0*(dh$V1)[16385:32768],(dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of filtered spliced F(w)_EP1+F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=1,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768],0*(dk$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=4)
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transforms of both Riemann-Siegel Euler Product components.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.8)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768],(dk$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=4,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_EP1",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
plot.new()
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((df$V1)[16385:32768],1*(df$V1)[1:16384]),col="darkblue",typ="l",
     xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_Z_zeta",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.8,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
abline(v=c(-20,20)*pi,col=6,lty=2)
mtext("True fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function for the interval s=(0.25+I*25320.9,0.25+I*26959.2)",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.75)
plot.new()
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of filtered F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend="NEW splicing method",bg="white",xjust=0)
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of spliced filtered F(w)_EP2 + \n scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP2)",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.5,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5))
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transform of the second Riemann-Siegel Euler Product component and its scaled mirror conjugate.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.62)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP2)",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c(expression(F[-w] == bar(F[w])%*% abs(~chi(1-(0.25+I%*%2%*%pi%*%exp(2%*%w))) )),""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.6)


```






```{r Zfnzetafunctionoveraninterval_anditsfouriertransform_26140_pm819_resp05, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="A comparison of the \\textbf{real part} of the fourier transforms of the Riemann-Siegel Z functions of the Riemann Zeta function and partial Euler Product (using truncation at $N_1 = \\sqrt{\\frac{t}{2\\pi}}$) in the complex plane at $\\sigma=0.05$ for the interval t=26140+(-819.1,819.2). The first row shows an existing method to filter and splice together the \\textbf{real parts} of the two fourier transform components of the partial Euler Product approximation of the Riemann-Siegel Z function. This old method is not accurate for $\\sigma=0.05$ as can be seen with the difference in middle column result compared to the true fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function shown in the second row. The bottom row uses the zeroth order functional relationship between the fourier sidebands of the Riemann-Siegel Z function to provide a robust partial Euler Product approximation whereby (since $\\sigma=0.05 < 0.5$) only the \\textbf{real part} of the filtered second fourier transform component is spliced together with the \\textbf{real part} of a scaled mirror conjugate of itself.", out.width = c("99%"), fig.show = "hold",  fig.height=7.5, warning=FALSE}


df=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p05_26140_Z.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
df$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",df$V1));
df$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",df$V2));

dg=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p05_26140_zeta.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dg$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dg$V1));
dg$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dg$V2));

dh=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p05_26140_EP12b.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dh$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dh$V1));
dh$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dh$V2));

di=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p05_26140_fadjneg.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
di$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",di$V1));
di$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",di$V2));

dj=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p05_26140_fhEP12b.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dj$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dj$V1));
dj$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dj$V2));

dk=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p05_26140_EP12a.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dk$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dk$V1));
dk$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dk$V2));

par(mar = c(1.75, 2.05, 1.5, 1.5))
# plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1)),c(as.numeric(df$V1)[16385:32768],as.numeric(df$V1)[1:16384]),col="gray",typ="l",    xlim=c(-2500,2500),ylim=c(-3e5,3e5));grid(col="gray2")

layout(mat = matrix(c(1,2,3,
                      4,5,6,
                      7,8,9),
                    nrow = 3,
                    byrow = TRUE))

plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[16385:32768],(dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("old splicing method \n produces inaccurate result \n for Re(s)=0.05",""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.8)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c(0*(dh$V1)[16385:32768],(dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of filtered spliced F(w)_EP1+F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=1,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768],0*(dk$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=4)
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transforms of both Riemann-Siegel Euler Product components.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.8)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768],(dk$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=4,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_EP1",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("this fourier component is numerically unstable \n for Re(s)=0.05 due to very large magnitude \n oscillations in the input EP1 Euler Product spectra \n exceeding double precision limit",""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.6)
plot.new()
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((df$V1)[16385:32768],1*(df$V1)[1:16384]),col="darkblue",typ="l",
     xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_Z_zeta",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.8,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
abline(v=c(-20,20)*pi,col=6,lty=2)
mtext("True fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function for the interval s=(0.25+I*25320.9,0.25+I*26959.2)",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.75)
plot.new()
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of filtered F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend="NEW splicing method",bg="white",xjust=0)
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of spliced filtered F(w)_EP2 + \n scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP2)",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.5,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5))
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transform of the second Riemann-Siegel Euler Product component and its scaled mirror conjugate.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.62)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP2)",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c(expression(F[-w] == bar(F[w])%*% abs(~chi(1-(0.05+I%*%2%*%pi%*%exp(2%*%w))) )),""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.6)


```



```{r Zfnzetafunctionoveraninterval_anditsfouriertransform_26140_pm819_resp0, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="A comparison of the \\textbf{real part} of the fourier transforms of the Riemann-Siegel Z functions of the Riemann Zeta function and partial Euler Product (using truncation at $N_1 = \\sqrt{\\frac{t}{2\\pi}}$) in the complex plane at $\\sigma=0.0$ for the interval t=26140+(-819.1,819.2). The first row shows an existing method to filter and splice together the \\textbf{real parts} of the two fourier transform components of the partial Euler Product approximation of the Riemann-Siegel Z function. This old method is not accurate for $\\sigma=0.0$ as can be seen with the difference in middle column result compared to the true fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function shown in the second row. The bottom row uses the zeroth order functional relationship between the fourier sidebands of the Riemann-Siegel Z function to provide a robust partial Euler Product approximation whereby (since $\\sigma=0.0 < 0.5$) only the \\textbf{real part} of the filtered second fourier transform component is spliced together with the \\textbf{real part} of a scaled mirror conjugate of itself.", out.width = c("99%"), fig.show = "hold",  fig.height=7.5, warning=FALSE}


df=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p0_26140_Z.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
df$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",df$V1));
df$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",df$V2));

dg=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p0_26140_zeta.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dg$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dg$V1));
dg$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dg$V2));

dh=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p0_26140_EP12b.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dh$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dh$V1));
dh$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dh$V2));

di=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p0_26140_fadjneg.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
di$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",di$V1));
di$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",di$V2));

dj=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p0_26140_fhEP12b.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dj$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dj$V1));
dj$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dj$V2));

dk=read.csv("/home/john/pari/vhat_p0_26140_EP12a.csv",header=FALSE,sep=",",colClasses = "character")
dk$V1=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dk$V1));
dk$V2=as.numeric(gsub(" ","",dk$V2));

par(mar = c(1.75, 2.05, 1.5, 1.5))

layout(mat = matrix(c(1,2,3,
                      4,5,6,
                      7,8,9),
                    nrow = 3,
                    byrow = TRUE))

plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[16385:32768],(dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("old splicing method \n produces inaccurate result \n for Re(s)=0.0",""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.8)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c(0*(dh$V1)[16385:32768],(dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of filtered spliced F(w)_EP1+F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=1,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768],0*(dk$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=4)
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transforms of both Riemann-Siegel Euler Product components.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.8)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dk$V1)[16385:32768],(dk$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=4,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_EP1",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c("this fourier component is numerically unstable \n for Re(s)=0.0 due to very large magnitude \n oscillations in the input EP1 Euler Product spectra \n exceeding double precision limit",""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.6)
plot.new()
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((df$V1)[16385:32768],1*(df$V1)[1:16384]),col="darkblue",typ="l",
     xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="",ylab="real part of F(w)_Z_zeta",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.8,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
abline(v=c(-20,20)*pi,col=6,lty=2)
mtext("True fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function for the interval s=(0.0+I*25320.9,0.0+I*26959.2)",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.75)
plot.new()
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of filtered F(w)_EP2",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend="NEW splicing method",bg="white",xjust=0)
plot(c(0:(ceiling(32768/2)-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dh$V1)[1:16384]),typ="l",col=3,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of spliced filtered F(w)_EP2 + \n scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP2)",cex.axis=0.6,cex.main=0.5,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
lines(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5))
mtext("Splicing together the filtered fourier transform of the second Riemann-Siegel Euler Product component and its scaled mirror conjugate.",side=3,line=0.5,cex=0.62)
plot(c(-floor(32768/2):(-1))*2*pi/32768/.05,c((dj$V1)[16385:32768]),typ="l",col=5,xlim=c(-2,2)*pi,ylim=c(-1e5,1e5),main="",xlab="w - sideband angular frequency (rad/sec) ",ylab="real part of scaled filtered conj(F(w)_EP2)",cex.axis=0.6,mgp = c(0.7, 0.2, 0),tck=-.02,cex.lab=0.7);grid(col="gray2")
legend(x="topleft",legend=c(expression(F[-w] == bar(F[w])%*% abs(~chi(1-(0.05+I%*%2%*%pi%*%exp(2%*%w))) )),""),bg="white",xjust=0,cex=0.6)


```




\clearpage

## Comparing NEW algorithm based partial Euler Product spectral filtering and splicing calculations of extreme peak heights along $s=1+I\cdot t$ to known peak heights

In [11,12] the n=32768 point fourier grid with $\Delta t=0.05$ was found to be sufficient for 3+ decimal accuracy with respect to grid search of extreme Riemann Zeta peaks on the critical line. Using the NEW algorithm for generating a partial Euler Product based spectral filtering and splicing approximation of the fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function off the critical line, followed by inverse fourier transformation, Table 1 below compares 

(i) the NEW algorithm accuracy for extreme peak height estimation away from the real axis within a given spectral sample $t=t_0+(-819.1,819.2)$,

(ii) the NEW algorithm accuracy for extreme peak height estimation away from the real axis \textbf{when the extreme peak is in the centre} of the spectral sample being fourier analysed,

(iii) the magnitude of the Riemann Zeta function at the identified peak position

\begin{equation}
|\zeta(s)| = |e^{I\cdot imag(\theta_{ext}(s))}\zeta(s)|
\end{equation}

(iv) the magnitude of the tapered truncation at the second quiescent region for Riemann Zeta Dirichlet Series $\left\lfloor\frac{t}{\pi}\right\rfloor$ approximation

\begin{align}
e^{I\cdot imag(\theta_{ext}(s))}\zeta(s)
&\approx e^{I\cdot imag(\theta_ext(s))}\cdot\Bigg[\sum_{k=1}^{\left(\lfloor\frac{t}{\pi}\rfloor-p\right)}\left(\frac{1}{k^{(s)}}\right) +\sum_{i=\left(-p+1\right)}^{p}\frac{\frac{1}{2^{2p}} \left( 2^{2p}-\sum_{k=1}^{i+p}\binom{2p}{2p-k}\right)}{\left( (\lfloor\frac{t}{\pi}\rfloor+i)^{(s)}\right)}\Bigg] \nonumber \\
&,  \qquad \Im(s) \rightarrow \infty
\end{align}

(v) the magnitude of the tapered truncation at the first quiescent region for Riemann Zeta Dirichlet Series $\left\lfloor\sqrt{\frac{t}{2\pi}}\right\rfloor$ approximation

\begin{align}
e^{I\cdot imag(\theta_{ext}(s))}\zeta(s)
&\approx e^{I\cdot imag(\theta_ext(s))}\cdot\Bigg[\sum_{k=1}^{\left(\lfloor\sqrt{\frac{t}{2\pi}}\rfloor-p\right)}\left(\frac{1}{ k^{(s)}}\right) +\sum_{i=\left(-p+1\right)}^{p}\frac{\frac{1}{2^{2p}} \left( 2^{2p}-\sum_{k=1}^{i+p}\binom{2p}{2p-k}\right)}{\left( (\lfloor\sqrt{\frac{t}{2\pi}}\rfloor+i)^{(s)}\right)} \nonumber \\
&+ \mychi(s)\cdot\Bigg(\sum_{k=1}^{\left(\lfloor\sqrt{\frac{t}{2\pi}}\rfloor-p\right)}\left(\frac{1}{ k^{(1-(s))}}\right) +\sum_{i=\left(-p+1\right)}^{p}\frac{\frac{1}{2^{2p}} \left( 2^{2p}-\sum_{k=1}^{i+p}\binom{2p}{2p-k}\right)}{\left( (\lfloor\sqrt{\frac{t}{2\pi}}\rfloor+i)^{(1-(s))}\right)}\Bigg)\Bigg] \nonumber \\
&,  \qquad \Im(s) \rightarrow \infty
\end{align}

\clearpage

```{r grid_results_high_t, echo = FALSE,results = "asis", warning=FALSE} 


table_grid_results <- as.data.frame(t(cbind(c("1550.000000","3.81427349447277009026177975012395686430","3.82098147266946298513433937840428486060","3.8209838739756127237948324067439304325271277061277","3.8209838739756127237948324067439304325271584627085","NA"),
c("6925.650000000000000","4.2773760728572830456601738267522583703","4.2773543212666240534477652405731518666754450488018","4.2773442646975451046824721786196846954195827066742","4.2773442646975451046824721786196846954195889396867","NA"),
c("26296.900000000000","4.5574628174494795719122188871121978341","4.5574670966689239807364690601664918787","4.5574680258665123569578559562192740682519007435177","4.5574680258665123569578559562192740682518955735448","4.5521032113915580119881973344542598186"),
c("169482.90000000000000000","4.9005026086992034270673467101401986755","4.9008243360367104822015507450235654935","4.9008275450501695124083614004064418867124261657061","4.9008275450501695124083614004064418867124254365690","4.9025370064657944920712538374650128215004880399121"),
c("6820051.00000000","5.4081902619865721986678623219534479100","5.4081380515393187224534844162659543851","NA","5.4081342277595713129426940538474247188419805488234","5.4081342564821392949386784538436256497733297801067"),
c("363991205.17884","6.5874214207540373881068647596462073514","6.5874302616031437456237705988207225394","NA","6.5874323368077774601084287955172252229283839001500","6.5874920563487529758857070076184971822406998959080"))))

colnames(table_grid_results) = c("t co-ordinate of peak", 
"filtered EP |estimate|  peak NOT centred in the input spectrum","filtered EP |estimate|  peak centred in input spectrum", "|zeta(1+I*t)|","tapered Dirichlet series |estimate|","tapered RS Dirichlet series |estimate|")
# for (i in 1:length(colnames(table_grid_results))) {
#   table_grid_results[,i] <- round(as.numeric(table_grid_results[,i]),26)
# }

for (i in 1:length(colnames(table_grid_results))) {
  table_grid_results[,i] = substr(table_grid_results[,i],1,10)
}

knitr::kable(table_grid_results,  caption = "A comparison of fourier transform filtered Euler Product estimate of extreme peak using NEW algorithm (i) when the peak is arbitrarily located in the input spectrum, (ii) the peak is centred in the input spectrum, (iii) $|\\zeta(1+I\\cdot t)|$, and 128 point tapered Dirichlet Series estimate based peak heights using (iv) truncation at the second quiescent region $N_2=\\left\\lfloor\\frac{t}{\\pi}\\right\\rfloor$ and (iv) at the first quiescent region $N_1=\\left\\lfloor\\sqrt{\\frac{t}{2\\pi}}\\right\\rfloor$",
             align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r","r"),longtable=TRUE)



```

Note that (i) the $N_1$ dirichlet estimate in the righthand column is NA when $t < 64^2*2*Pi=25735.93$ due to its 128 point tapering constraint and (ii) $|\zeta(s)|$ estimate is NA, i.e. unavailable due to the slow computation speed of the high precision method at higher t.

As can be seen from table 1, 

\begin{itemize}
\item the magnitude of the tapered truncation at the second quiescent region for Riemann Zeta Dirichlet Series $\left\lfloor\frac{t}{\pi}\right\rfloor$ approximation column 5 is in multidecimal place agreement with $|\zeta(1+I\cdot t)|$
\item if the extreme peak is at the centre of the input spectrum sample (see column 3) then the NEW algorithm for generating a partial Euler Product based spectral filtering and splicing approximation of the fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function off the critical line, followed by inverse fourier transformation has 4+ decimal place agreement with $\zeta(1+\cdot t)|$ as a benefit of lower error for the centre of a fourier transform (since it is an interpolation method)
\item if the extreme peak is otherwise located within the input spectrum sample then the agreement with $\zeta(1+I\cdot t)|$ is poorer (2-3+decimal places) but remains useful for the NEW algorithm for generating a partial Euler Product based spectral filtering and splicing approximation of the fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function off the critical line, followed by inverse fourier transformation
\item comparing columns 3 and 7, the NEW algorithm for generating a partial Euler Product based spectral filtering and splicing approximation of the fourier transform of the Riemann-Siegel Z function off the critical line, followed by inverse fourier transformation can be a better estimate of $\zeta(1+\cdot t)|$ than the tapered truncation at the first quiescent region for Riemann Zeta Dirichlet Series $\left\lfloor\sqrt{\frac{t}{2\pi}}\right\rfloor$ approximation
\end{itemize}


## Conclusions 


The above spectral filtering and a revised method of rescaled phase conjugate reflection based on a zeroth order approximation for the functional behaviour between fourier transform Riemann-Siegel Z function sidebands based on only one partial Euler Product fourier component (see equations (36) and (37)), using truncation lengths of $N_1 +\Delta$ and subsequent inverse fourier transformation results in more robust and quicker partial Euler Product based approximations of the Riemann-Siegel Z function (away from the real axis) off the critical line. 



\newpage

## References

1. Edwards, H.M. (1974). Riemann's zeta function. Pure and Applied Mathematics 58. New York-London: Academic Press. ISBN 0-12-232750-0. Zbl 0315.10035.

2. Riemann, Bernhard (1859). "?ber die Anzahl der Primzahlen unter einer gegebenen Gr?sse". Monatsberichte der Berliner Akademie.. In Gesammelte Werke, Teubner, Leipzig (1892), Reprinted by Dover, New York (1953).

3. Titchmarsh, E.C. (1986) The Theory of the Riemann Zeta Function. 2nd Revised (Heath-Brown, D.R. ) Edition, Oxford University Press, Oxford.

4. Berry, M. V. "The Riemann-Siegel Expansion for the Zeta Function: High Orders and Remainders." Proc. Roy. Soc. London A 450, 439-462, 1995.

5. The PARI~Group, PARI/GP version 2.12.0, Univ. Bordeaux, 2018,
\url{http://pari.math.u-bordeaux.fr/}.

6. Martin, J.P.D. "A useful approximation of the Riemann Zeta function away from the real axis using spectral filtering of the partial Euler product" (2025)
http://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.28528652

7. Martin, J.P.D. "Crucial steps for robust first quiescent region truncated partial Euler Product based approximations of closely spaced Riemann Zeta function non-trivial zeroes" (2025)
http://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.29095232

8. Martin, J.P.D. "Examples of spectrally filtered first quiescent region truncated partial Euler Product based approximations of Riemann Siegel Z functions for different L-functions and the two Davenport-Heilbronn 5-periodic functions on the critical line away from the real axis" (2025)
http://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.29577545

9. Martin, J.P.D. "For L-functions without poles spectrally filtered first quiescent region truncated partial Euler Product based estimates of Riemann-Siegel Z functions can provide useful standalone approximations about the real axis across the critical strip." (2025)
https://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.29825609

10. Martin, J.P.D. "A first order approximation about the real axis across the critical strip of the continuous version of $\sqrt{\zeta(s)\zeta(1-s)}$ using a spectrally filtered linear combination of the first quiescent region truncated partial Euler Product and the simple s=1 pole." (2025)
https://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.29991979

11. Martin, J.P.D. "Evidence of a sharp multi-modal distribution in the relative position of the highest Riemann Zeta peaks along the critical line in the piecewise intervals $[N^2\cdot 2\pi,(N+1)^2\cdot 2\pi),N\in\mathbb{Z}$ and a possible explanation using a heuristic approximation on the critical line of the indefinite integral of the Riemann Siegel Z function." (2025) https://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.30740681

12. Martin, J.P.D. "Evidence of a sharp multi-modal distribution in the relative position of the highest Riemann Siegel Z function analogue peaks of 5 periodic 1st degree L-functions and Davenport Heilbronn functions, along the critical line in the piecewise intervals $[N^2\cdot \frac{2\pi}{5},(N+1)^2\cdot \frac{2\pi}{5}),N\in\mathbb{Z}$." (2026) https://dx.doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.30992710

13. R Core Team (2017). R: A language and environment for statistical computing. R Foundation for
Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria. \url{https://www.R-project.org/}.

14. RStudio Team (2015). RStudio: Integrated Development for R. RStudio, Inc., Boston, MA URL
http://www.rstudio.com/.
